EIGHTEENTH EPISODE
THE VENGEANCE OF WU FANG.
Elaine was still la the power of Wu
Fan?. Kennedy had thwarted the
Chinese master criminal In his search
fur the millions amassed by the
'hitching Hand. Hut any Joy that he
might have derived from this success
was completely obscured by the fear
hat Wu might wreak some diabolical
vengeance on Elaine.
Nor were Kennedy's fears unfounded.
Wu and Long Sin had scarcely reach
ed the secret apartment when the
mbtle Chinaman began to contem-I-!ate
revenge
Long Sin was Fmoktng a Chinese
pipe, resting after their hurried flight,
while Wu, the tireloss, was seated at
n table at the other end of the room.
t last Wu Fang took up n long Chi
nese dirk from the table before him.
looked at it, turned it over, felt its
edge He rose nnd deliberately walked
arrows to a door leading into a back
room.
On a couch lay Elaine, and Vlth her,
as a guardian, was Weepy Mary.
As Wu, the Serpent, entered and
advanced slowly toward Elaine, she
crouched back from him in deadly
-ar.
Slowly he drew from under his robe
t'.ie Chinese dirk. He felt the edge of
n again and gazed significantly at
Elaine.
It was a critical moment.
.lust then Long Sin entered. "One
cf the five millions walls outside," he
reported simply, with a bow.
Wu understood. It had been a pleas
ant fiction of his that although he did
not, of course, absolutely control such
a stupendous organization, he could,
by his subtle power, force almost un
limited allegiance from the simple
coolies in that district of China from
which he came.
Out in the front room, just a mo
ment before, a knock at the door had
disturbed Ixmg Sin, and a Chinese
servant had announced a visitor. Long
Sin had waved to the servant to usher
him In, and a poorly clad coolie ba
entered.
Ho bowed as Long Sin faced him.
"Where is the master?" he had asked.
Loug Sin had not deigned to speak.
With a mere wave of his hand he in
dicated that he would be the bearer
of the message, and had followed Wu
through the door of the back room.
So, almost by chance, Wu was Inter
rupted in the brutal vengeance which
had first come to his mind. He
sheathed the knife and, still without
a word, went back Into the main room,
giving a nod to Weepy Mary to guard
Elaine closely.
Wu eyed the coolie until the new
comer could almost feel the master's
penetrating gaze, although his head
was bowed in awe. Quickly the coolie
thrust his hand under his blouse and
drew forth a package. With another
bow he advanced.
"For your enemies, O master," he
said, handing the package over to
Wu.
The coolie started to open the pack
age, removed the paper wrapper, and
then a silk wrapping inside. Finally
he came to a box, from which he drew
a leather pouch, each operation con
ducted with greater care as It became
evident that the contents were espe
cially precious in some way. Then he
took from the pouch a small vial.
"What is it?" demanded Wu Fang,
as the coolie displayed it.
The coolie draw forth now a magni
fying glass and a glass slide. Opening
the vial with great care he shook
something out on the slide, then
placed it under the lens.
"Look!" ho said simply.
Wu bent over and looked. Under
the lens what had formerly seemed to
be merely a black speck of dirt be
came now one of the most weird and
uncanny little creatures to be found In
all the realm of nature. It seemed to
be all legs and feelers moving at once.
A normal person would have looked
at the creature only with the greatest
repugnance. Wu regarded it with a
tort of unholy fascination.
"And it Is?" he queried.
"What the white man calls the Afri
can tick which carries the recurrent
fever," answered the coolie deferen
tially. A flash of intense exultation seemed
to darken Wu Fang's Blnlster face.
"Walt," he said to the coolie, as he
PRAISES THE "CORN DODGER"
Wife of Poatmaater-General Give
Recipe for Food Which She
Declare Delicious.
In the interests of economy and con
serving the food supply, the German
housewife has been told to mis rye
and potato flour with wheat In mak
ing bread, remarks Frances Frear in
Leslie's. In view ot the excessive
amount of wheat the United States
has exported to the warring nations,
moved slowly aguln Into tho liack
room.
1 jinx Sin hud remained There. With
Weepy Mary ha wua guarding Elaine
when Wu Fang re-entered. Elaine
was thoroughly arouaed by tlilu time.
"Suffering la a s4ate of mind," Wu
said in a low tone, "and I have do
elded that it would bo poor revenge
for me to harm you. You are free.'
Nothing could have come aa a greut
er surprise to Elaine.
"Do you do you really mean it?'
she asked, scarcely able to believe
what her eara heard.
Wu merely nodded, and with a wave
of hla band to Long Sin Indicated that
Eluine waa to be released.
She nerved herself to take several
steps toward the door. She had
scarcely crosHed half the room.
"Walt!" ordered Wu sharply.
Was he merely torturing her, as
cat might torture a mouse? She
stopped obediently, afraid to look at
him.
"This will be the vengeance of Wu
rang," he went on impressively
"Slowly, one by one, your friends w ill
weaken and die, then your family, un
til finally only you are left. Then will
come your turn."
Ho stopped again and raised his
long, lean forefinger. "Co," he hissed.
"I wish you much Joy."
5
I don't think that in all our friend'
ship I have ever seen Kennedy so ut
terly depressed as he was when we
returned after the discovery of the
vast fortune which Bennett had clev
erly secreted.
There seemed to be absolutely noth
ing to do until Bome new clue turned
up. I placed my hand on his shoulder,
but the words that would encourage
hint died on my lips. Several times 1
started to speak, but each time
checked myself. There did not seem
to be anything that would be appro
priate for such an occasion.
A sharp ring at the telephone made
both of us fairly jump, so nervous
had we become. Kennedy reached
over instantly for the instrument in
the vague hope that at last there was
some news.
As I watched his face it changed
first from despair to wonder, and final
ly It seemed to light up with the mont
remarkable look of relief and happi
ness that one could imagine.
"I shall be right over," he cried,
jamming the receiver down on the
hook, and in the same motion reach
ing for his hat and coat. "Walter,"
he cried, "It is Elaine! They have 1st
her go!"
I seized my own hat and coat fn
time to follow him, and we dashed out
of the laboratory.
The suspense under which Aunt
Josephine had been living had told on
He Felt the Edge of the Dagger and
Gazed Significantly at Elaine.
her. Her niece, Elaine's cousin, Mary
Iirown, who lived at Rockledge, h.d
come into the city to comfort Aunt
Josephine, and they had been sitting
that morning in the library.
Suddenly a startled cry from Jen
nings out In the hall caused both la
dles to jump to their feet. They could
scarcely believe what they heard as
the faithful old butler cried out the
name.
"Why, Miss Elaine!" he gasped.
An Instant later Elaine herself burst
into the room and flung herself into
Aunt Josephine's arms. All talking
and half crying from joy at once, they
crowded about her.
In the excitement Aunt Josephine
had seized the telephone and called
our number. She did not even wait to
break the good news, but handed tho
telephone to Elaine herself.
We left the laboratory on the run,
too fast to notice that just around the
building line at the comer stood a lim
ousine with shades drawn. Even if
we bad paused to glance back we
and the consequent rise In the price
of flour, the American housewife may
soon have to look for a substitute for
high-priced wheat bread. We have
the advantage ot Europe In possess
ing another great staple corn, and
the high price of wheat will doubt
less mean the spread of the popular
ity of corn bread, from its native
southland, throughout the country.
Mrs. Burleson, wife of the postmaster
general, sees nothing to be dreaded in
this, and gives the recipe tor the "corn
dodger," one of her favorites, which
t -V c -J I
r
could not have teen Wu Fang arl
Ijoag Sin lnrHe gazing out tb-rough
the corner of faxi curtains. They were
In European Areas now, and had evi
dently come prepared for just what
they knew was likely to happen.
We were admitted by the faithful
Jennings and almost ran luto the library.
"Oh, Craig!" cried Elaine, as Ken
nedy, almost speechless, seized her by
both hands.
For a few seconds none of us could
speak. Then followed a veritable flood
of eager conversation,
"Won't you come and stay with
me a few days up In the country,
dear?" urged Mnry at last.
Elaine thought a moment, then
turned to Aunt Josephine.
"yes." considered her aunt, "I think
It would do you good."
Still she hesitated, then shyly looked
at Kennedy and laughed. "You, too,
Craig, must be fugged out." she said,
frankly. "Come up there with us and
take a rest."
Kennedy smiled. "I shall be de
lighted," ho accepted promptly.
We chatted, then left a few mo
ments later bo that Kennedy could
pack.
a
Around the corner from the labora
tory, aa we dashed, out, had been, aa
I have said, Wu Fang and Ixng Sin
looking out from the limousine. No
sooner had we disappeared across the
campus than their driver started up
the car and they sped around to our
apartment.
Cautiously they alighted and walked
down the street. Then, maklprf sure
they were not observed, they entered
and mounted the stairs to our door
way. Long Sin was stationed down
the hall on guard, while Wu Fang drew
from his pocket a blank key, a file and
a candle. He lighted the candle and
held the key in its flame until it was
covered with soot.
Then he Inserted the key into the
keyhole, turned it and took the key
out. He filed the'key, trying It again
and again. Finally he finished and
opened the door. Heckoning Long Sin,
he entered our room.
As they stood there Wu Fang gazed
about our living room keenly. He was
evidently considering where to place
something.
Finally his eye rested on the tele
phone It seemed to suggest an idea
to him and he crossed over to It. Care
fully holding down the receiver on the
hook, he unscrewed the case which
holds the diaphragm, while with his
clever fingers he held the rest of the
Instrument Intact. Then he removed
from his pocket the vial which the
coolie had given him and placed its
contents on the diaphragm itself.
Quickly now he replaced the receiver
and, having finished their work. Long
Sin and Wu Fang stealthily crept out.
A second time, as we approached
our apartment after the visit to Elaine,
we were too excited to notice the
limousine In which were Wu and Long
Sin. But no sooner had we entered
than Long Sin left the car with a
final word of Instruction from his
master.
Upstairs, In the apartment, Kennedy
began hurriedly to pack, and I helped
him as well as I could. We were in
the midst of it when the telephone
rang and I answered It.
"Hello!" I called.
There was no response.
"Hello, hello!" I repeated, raising
my voice.
Still there was no answer. I worked
the hook up and down, but could get
no reply, and finally, disgusted, hung
up.
A moment later, I recall now, ft
seemed to me as though some one had
stuck a pin Into the lobe of my ear.
We had scarcely got back to work
when the telephone bell jangled again.
and a second time I answered It.
'Is Mr. Kennedy there?" came back
a strange voice.
I handed the instrument to Craig.
"Hello!" he called. "Who is this?"
No response.
"Hello, hello!" he shouted, working
the hook as I had done and, in my
case, there was still no answer.
Neither of us thought anything of
it at the time, but now I recall that I
did see Kennedy once or twice press
the lobe of bis ear as though some
thing had hurt it.
We did not know until later that in
pay station down the street our
arch enemy, Long Sin, had been call
ing us up and then, with a wicked
smile, refusing to speak to us.
It was about a week later that I
came home late one night from the
Star, feeling pretty done up. What
ever it was, a violent fever seemed
to have come on me suddenly. The
next morning when I woke up I felt
a lassitude that made it quite hard
enough even to lounge about in my
bathrobe.- Finally, feeling no better,
decided to see a doctor. I put on
my clothes with a decided effort and
went out.
The nearest doctor was about half
a block away and we scarcely knew
him, for neither Kennedy nor I are
exactly sickly.
He sounded me and pounded me,
talked a little while on generalities,
diet and exercise, then walked over
to a cabinet, and emptied out a few
pills into a little paper box.
Take one every hour," he said.
"Cut your cigarettes to three a day,
and don't drink coffee. Four dollars,
please.'
I paid him and went back to the
apartment, my head soon in a whirl
from a new onset of the fever.
I managed to get back into my bath
robe, and threw myself down on the
divan, propped up with pillows. I had
taken the pills, but they had no more
effect than sugar or milk. By this
she declares to be more healthful than
white bread. "Measure and scald the
meal," says Mrs. Burleson. "Pour
just enough boiling water over the
meal to moisten it Add salt accord
ing to the quantity of cornmeal used,
then a tablespoonful or two of cot
tonseed oil I always use the oil
and mix the batter with enough cold
water to spread a little or mold it
in small dodgers in the hands. This
should be baked In the oven on a well
greased griddle, or flattened into
rakes and cooked on top of the stove
time I was much more deilrtvsa and
was crying out.
I saw fuces about me, but I did not
see the faces which were actuully out
by our hall door. Wu Fang and Long
Sin had waited patiently for their re
veuge. Now thut they thought suftl
cleut time had elupsed, they had sto
len silently to "the apartment door.
While Ling Sin watched. Wu listened.
"The whfte devil has It," whispered
Wu Fang, aa ho rejoined his fellow
conspirator.
How long I should have remained
In this state, and In fact how long I
did remain, I don't know. Vaguely, I
recall that our acquaintance, Johnson,
who had the apartment acroaa the
hall, at last heard my cries anil came
iu.
Somehow or other I knew the state
I was in. I knew it was Johnson, yet
It all seemed unreal to me. With a
greut effort I gathered all my scat
tered wita and managed to shout out,
"Telegraph Kennedy Rockledge."
a
At about tne same time, up at Rock
ledge, Kennedy and Elaine, with her
cousin, Mary Drown, were starting out
for a horseback ride through the hills.
They were chatting gayly, but Ken
nedy was forcing himself to do so.
In fact, they had scarcely gone half
a mile when Kennedy, who was rid
ing between the two and fighting off
by sheer nerve the illness he felt, sud
denly fell over In half a faint on the
f 1
I
..'.tvt -' A5; v
"Look!" Said Kennedy,
horse's neck. Elaine and Mary reined
up their horses.
"Why, Craig," cried Elaine, startled,
"what's the matter?"
The sound of her voice seemed to
arouse him. He braced up. "Oh, noth
ing, I guess," he said with a forced
smile. "I'm all right."
it was no use, however. They hud
to cut short the ride, and Kennedy re
turned to the house glad to drop down
in an easy chair on the porch, while
Elaine hovered about him solicitously.
They summoned the country doctor,
but it took him some time to get out
to the house. Suddenly a messenger
boy rode up on his bicycle and mount
ed the porch steps. "Telegram for
Mr. Kennedy," he announced, looking
about and picking out Craig naturally
as the person be wanted.
Kennedy nodded and took the yel
low envelope while Elaine signed tor
it. Listlessly he tore it open. It
read:
Uraig Kennedy,
Care Wellington Brown,
Rockledge, N. Y.
Jameson very ill. Wants you. Better
come. Johnson.
The message seemed to rouse Ken
nedy in spite of his fever. His lace
showed keen alarm, which he endeav
orded to conceal from Elaine. But her
quick eye had caught the look.
"I must see Walter," he exclaimed,
rising rather weakly and going into
the house.
How he ever did it is still, I think, a
mystery to him, but he managed to
pack up and, in spite of the alternating
fever and chills, make the journey
back to the city.
When at last Craig arrived at our
apartment, it must have seemed to
him that he found me 'almost at
death's door.
Ill himself. Kennedy threw himself
down for a moment, exhausted. "When
did this thing come on, Walter?" he
asked of Johnson.
"Yesterday, I think, as nearly as I
can find out," replied our friend.
Craig was decidedly worried.
"There's only one person in New York
to call on," he murmured, pulling him
self out of bed and getting into the
living room as best he could.
"Is that you, Godowskl?' he asked
over the telephone. "Well, doctor,
this Is Kennedy. Come over to my
apartment, quick. I've a case two
cases, for you."
Godowskl was a world-famous scien
tist in his line and had specialized in
bacteriology, mainly in tropical dis
eases. As Kennedy hung up the receiver
he made his way back again to the
bedroom, scratching his ear. He no
ticed that I was doing the same in
my delirium.
"Has Walter been scratching his
ear?" he asked of Johnson.
Johneson nodded. "That's strange,"
considered Craig, thoughtfully. "I've
been doing the same."
He turned back into the living room
and for a moment looked about. Fi
nally hla eye happened to fall on the
telephone and an idea seemed to oc
cur to him.
He went over to the instrument and
unscrewed the receiver. Carefully he
until a rich brown. With butter there
is nothing better." Southern wom
en contend that sugar, eggs and other
expensive Ingredients are not neces
sary to make good corn bread. ' They
ought to know, aa they have made
corn bread to perfection in the South
for many generations.
Geologists Needed In Armlet.
Since war has left the realm ot fiery
romance and become a matter of dig
ging the enemy out, of lead trenches,
approach trenches, sapping, under-
lorked inside. Then he looked closer.
Tttcre was something peculiar about It
und he picked up a blank sheet of
white paper, dusting off the dlphragm
on It. There, on the paper, were iu
nuuieruble little black specks.
Just then, outside, Doctor Godow
ski's car drew up and he jumped out
swinging hla black bag. Not being ac
attainted with what we were goins
through, Godowakl did not notice the
almond-eyed Chlnaninn who wu
watching down the street.
"How do you do, doctor?" greeted
Craig faintly, at the door.
"What seems to be the difficulty?'
Inquired the doctor eagerly.
"I don't kuow," returned Craig
"but I have my suspicions. I'm too ill
to verify them myself, so I've culled
on you. Look at Jameson drat," he
added.
While Godowskl was examining me
Crnlir managed to ret out hla micro
scope and was locking through It at
the strange black specks on the pa
per. There, under the lens, be could
see the most remarkable, almost mi
croscopic creature, all legs and feel
era, a most vicious object.
Weak though he was. he could not
help an exclamation of exultation at
his discovery, juat as Godowskl- had
finished with me.
''I-ook!" he crleil calling the doctor.
"I know what the trouble is, Godow
skl." He had started to tell, but the ex-
aiwSafc.' ;
Turning to His Microscope.
cltenient of the lourney and the exe"
tion were so great that he could hard
ly mumble.
'Here- look on this paper. ' he
cried. "From tho telephone "
He hud risen and was handing th
paper to the scientist when his weak
ness overcame him. He fell flat on his
face on the floor and dropped the pi
per, spilling the contents.
Godowski, now thoroughly alarmed,
bent over Craig. But the delirium
had overcome Kennedy, too.
Unable to make any sense out of
Craig's broken wanderings, Godowskl
tost no time in taking samples of our
blood.
Then he hurried away to his labora
tory in his car. As he did so, however,
Long Sin leaped into a taxicab which
was waiting, ind followed.
In Godowskl's laboratory, where he
was studying tropical diseases, the
bacteriologist set to work at once to
confirm his own growing suspicions.
From a monkey which he had there
for experimenal purposes, he drew off
some blood samples. Then, with the
aid of his assistant, he took the blood
samples he had obtained from
us. The monkey's blood, under the
microscope, seemed full of rather
elongated, wriggling germs, of a pe
culiar species. In and out they made
their way among the blood corpuscles,
each like a dart aimed at life itself.
Then he took the samples of our
blood. In them were the same germs
carried by that gruesome tick!
"The spirillum!" he muttered. "They
are Infected- with African recurrent
fever. The only remedy is atoxyl, ad
ministered intravenously, after the
manner of Professor Ehrllch's famous
'606.' "
Godowskl had rung the call box has
tily for a messenger, when Long Sin,
who had managed stealthily to creep
up to the doctor's laboratory window,
scowled through at the action then
moved away.
While his assistant gathered the ap
paratus, the doctor wrote:
Miss Anne Septix,
301 W. th St.:
Please go at once to the apartment
of Craig Kennedy, Claremont
Ave. Surgical case.
GODOWSKI, M. D.
The boy arrived finally and the doc
tor gave him a generous tip, to hurry
with the note.
He had turned the corner, however,
when Long Sin appeared. Subtly he
played on the boy's cupidity to get
him to deliver a note of his own
even offered to deliver the boy's note
for him. The flash of a five dollar bill
made the rest easy.
As the boy disappeared on a fake
errand. Long Sin, with the rear note,
hurried downtown, smiling wickedly.
"They have discovered the fever,
master," he reported in the den.
Wu was beside Imself with rage.
Before he could speak, however, Long
Sin spread out Godowskl's message.
"But I have this," he added.
It took merely a glance to suggest
to Wu a new plan of action. He rose
and moved quickly into the back room.
"Come," he ordered Weepy Mary.
"You must dress up as a nurse im-
ground barracks and other evolutions
of the spade and pickax, new problems
face the general Btaff. It Is becoming
gradually evident that to the various
branches of knowledge that go to
make up the science of strategy there
must be added another namely, ge
ology. It Is not enough to be able to
pick out a hillside or bit of meadow
land that will best lend itself to the
tactics planned. The commanding offi
cer, fighting now a war ot intrench
ments, must choose a position that
will also be dlggabl If beneath a d
mediately." Quickly she donned one
of the numerous disguises, while Wu
planned bis campaign.
"Here," he directed when she was
ready, handing her a little vial. "You
mu lit Infect every Instrument the doc
tor nses on Kennedy and Jameson
see?"
She nodded, anl a moment later was
on her way uptown.
a
Meanwhile GoiTowskl himself had
arrived at our apartment, iue& to tne
relief of our frlord Johnson, and was
unpacking his instruments.
Quickly he Improvised two operat
ing tables and placed one or us on
euch. Then, with bis assistant, he put
on hla while robe, mask, gloves and
other precautious for asopals, setting
out the apparatus for the Intravenous
administration or the drug that wouK
kill the spirillum.
They had li'.lahed their preparations
and were waiting for Miss Meptlx.
"She ought to be here now," muttered
Godowakl Impatiently, looking at his
watch.
Just then a cab drove up outside.
"Perhaps that Is she!" he exclaimed.
"It must' be."
A few moments later the door of
the apartment opened. His face showed
hla disappointment. It was a stranger.
"Miss Septix Is 111," she introduced,
"and sent me to take her place."
The doctor looked about. "Very
well, then," he said briskly, seeing his
preparations. "Are you ready to go
ahead?"
fcthe nodded and tUrew off the coat
that covered her Immaculate white
uniform.
The specialist plunged whole-heart
edly into tils work of saving us now.
"Hand me that needle, please, be
directed the false nurse.
She moved over to the table near
by and took It up, pausing only long
enough to dip It secretly into a vial
she carried with her.
"Please hurry," repeated the doctor.
She turned from the table and hand
ed it to hliu. He adjusted it and al
ready held it poised for the thrust
which was not to cure but to polsen
us further.
"Weepy Mary!" cried a frightened
voice at our door.
Elaine had been deeply alarmed by
the sudden Illness of Kennedy and the
message from Jameson. No sooner
bad Kennedy gone than it flashed over
her that Wu Fang had predicted some
thing like this.
The threat: " she exclaimed, seek
ing her cousin. "Mary, I must go to
the city right away."
On the next train, then, she had
been speeding back to New York.
One glance at the improvised hos
pital was enough to alarm her. Hut
the sight that had transfixed her was
of a woman whose face she remem
bered well, though Kennedy and 1 had
never seen her
"Please, Miss,' began Godowski's
assistant, trying to quiet Elaine, while
Godowski turned in vexation to his
work.
"No. no!" repeated Elaine. "This
woman is no nurse. She Is a crim
inal!" Godowskl paused. It was true he
did not know the woman. He gazed
from Elaine to Weepy Mary In doubt.
The game was up. Weepy Mary
dropped a piece of gauze whlch-she
had soaked in the solution from the
vial which Wu had given her and bolt
ed for the door.
So sudden was her flight that no
one was quick enough to stop her.
The excitement, more thun ever,
alarmed Elaine now. "Tell me," she ap
pealed to Doctor Godowskl, "what Is
the matter?"
"In some way," he replied quickly,
"they have become infected with the
bite of an African tick which carries
spirillum fever."
Godowski raised his hands in de
spair. "I was just nbout to start," he
cried. "Everything is ready. I can't
send for another nurse. Every min
ute counts."
Elaine had thrown off her coat and
hat. Her sleeves were up In a mo
ment, and before the doctor knew what
she was about she was scrubbing her
hands In the antiseptic wash.
"Only show me -vhat to do," she
cried. "I will be the nurse!"
Several days later, when he had re
covered sufficiently from the diaboli
cal attack that hod been made upon
us, Kennedy w as a lain at work In
the laboratory, whi'e I was writing.
Our speaking tutre sounded, and I
knew that it was Klaine and Aunt Jo
sephine. "How do you fel?" Inquired Elaine
anxiously, as she almost ran across
the laboratory to Craig.
"Fine!" he exaggerated, brightly.
"Really?" she repeated anxiously.
"Look!" he said, turning to his mi
croBcope. He turned some blcod from a test
tube in our electric incubator and
placed a drop on a tillde. It was some
of the blood Infected by the germs car
ried by the tick.
"That is how our blood looked be
fore the new nurse arrived," he smiled,
while Elaine looked at It In horror.
Then he pricked his arm and let a
drop smear on another slide.
"Now look at that perfectly nor
mal," he added.
"Oh, I'm so glad, ' she exclaimed ra
diantly. "Normal thanks to you. You saved
us. You were Just In time," cried Craig,
taking both her harids in his.
He was about to kiss her, when she
broke away. "Cral?," she whispered,
blushing and looking hastily at us.
Aunt Josephine and I could only
smile at the disgusted glance Craig
gave us, as he thrust his hands in
bis pockets and wished us a thousand
miles away at that moment.

"Yes. And I'd like to be one qf the

Skulls as Drinking Cups.
The old Scandinavian sages repre
sent as among the delights of the Im
mortals the felicity of feasting and
drinking to drunkenness from the
skulls of the fops they had vanquished
on earth. Mandeville goes further, and
represents the Guebres as exposing
the dead bodies of their parents to the
fowls of the air until nothing but the
skeletons remained, and preserving
the Hkulls to be used as drinking cups.
What Interested Him.
Uncle Ben Barnstraw's city nephew
took him into a fire station one night
just in time to see the Are luddies
shinning like squirrels down a pole
from their quarters overhead. "Come
on, uncle, we'll go to the fire," the
nephew suggested. "I don't want to
go," replied the uncle. "I want to
stay here until they come back, so I
can see them scoot back up the pole
again." Judge.
Not in His Line.
"What do you intend to do now that
you're through college?" asked the
young B. A.'s father.
"I'm thinking of taking a post
graduate course in economics," replied
his son.
"Economics, hey?" said the father.
"If that's got anything to do with
economy, I don't see what makes you
think you've got any special aptitude
for the study."
SHE QUIT
But It Was a Hard Pull.
It Is hard to believe that coffee will
put a person in such a condition as it
did an Ohio woman. She tells het
own story:
"1 did not believe coffee caused my
trouble, and frequently said I liked it
so well I would not, and could not,
quit drinking it, but I was a miserable
sufferer from heart trouble and nenr
ous prostration for four years.
"I was scarcely able to be around,
had no energy and did not care for
anything. Was emaciated and had a
constant pain around my heart until I
thought I could not endure it.
"Frequently I had nervous chills
and the least excitement would drive
sleep away, and any little noise would
upset me terribly. I was gradually
getting worse until finally I asked my
self what's the use of being sick all
the time and buying medicine so that
I could indulge myself In coffee?
"So I got some Postum to help me
quit, I made it strictly according to
directions and I want to tell you that
change was the greatest step In my
life. It was easy to quit coffee be
cause I now like Postum better than
the coffee.
"One by one the old troubles left
until now I am In splendid health,
nerves steady, heart all right and the
pain all gone. Never have any more
nervous chills, don't take any medi
cine, can do all my house work and
have done a great deal besides."

text:
The Exploits of Elaine
A Detective Novel and a Motion Picture Drama
By ARTHUR B. REEVE
The Well-Known Nocellst and the
Creator of the ' 'Craig Kennedy' ’ Stories
Presented in Collaboration With the Pathe Players and the Eclectic Film Company
Copyright, I9H, by the Star Company All Foreign Rights Resenred
SYNOPSIS
The New York police are mystified by a
series of murders and other crimes. The
principal clue to the criminal is the warn
ing letter which is sent the victims, signed
with a "clutching hand.” The latest vic
tim of the mysterious assassin is Taylor
l>odge, the Insurance president His
daughter. Elaine, employs Craig Kennedy,
the famous scientific detective, to try to
unravel the mystery. What Kennedy ac
complishes is told by his friend Jameson,
a newspaper man. After many fruitless
attempts to put Elaine and Craig Ken
nedy out of the way the Clutching Hand
is at last found to be none other than
Perry Bennett, Elaine’s lawyer and the
man she is engaged to marry. Bennett
flees to the den of one of his Chinese
criminals. The Chinaman forces from
Bennett the secret of the whereabouts of
S7.ono.000. Then he gives the lawyer a
potion which will suspend animation for
months. Kennedy reaches Bennett’s side
Just after he has lost consciousness.
EIGHTEENTH EPISODE
THE VENGEANCE OF WU FANG.
Elaine was still in the power of Wu
Fang- Kennedy had thwarted the
Chinese master criminal in his search
for the millions amassed by tho
Clutching Hand, But any joy that he
might have derived from this success
was completely obscured by the fear
that Wu might wreak some diabolical
vengeance on Elaine.
Nor were Kennedy's fears unfounded.
Wu and I.ong Sin had scarcely reach
ed the secret apartment when the
subtle Chinaman began to contem
plate revenge.
Long Sin was smoking a Chinese
pipe, resting after their hurried flight,
while Wu, the tireless, was seated at
a table at the other end of the room.
At last Wu Fang took up a long Chi
nese dirk from the table before him,
looked at it, turned it over, felt its
edge. He rose and deliberately walked
across to a door leading into a back
room.
On a couch lay Elaine, and with her,
as a guardian, was Weepy Mary.
As Wu, the Serpent, entered and
ndvanced slowly toward Elaine, sho
crouched back from him in deadly
fear.
Slowly he drew from under his robe
the Chinese dirk. He felt the edge of
it again and gazed significantly at
Elaine.
It was a critical moment.
.lust then Long Sin entered. "One
of the five millions waits outside,” he
reported simply, with a bow.
Wu understood. It had been a pleas
ant fiction of his that although he did
not, of course, absolutely control such
a stupendous organization, he could,
by his subtle power, force almost un
limited allegiance from the simple
coolies in that district of China from
which he came.
Out in the front room, just a mo
m on t Vtof nrn n lrnnnlr of th« tlonr h o H
disturbed lx>ng Sin, and a Chinese
servant had announced a visitor. Long
Sin had waved to the servant to usher
him in, and a poorly clad coolie had
entered.
He bowed as Ixing Sin faced him.
"Where is the master?" he had asked.
Long Sin had not deigned to speak.
With a mere wave of his hand he in
dicated that he would be the bearer
of the message, and had followed Wu
through the door of the back room.
So, almost by chance, Wu was inter
rupted in the brutal vengeance which
had first come to his mind. He
sheathed the knife and, still without
a word, went back into the main room,
giving a nod to Weepy Mary to guard
Elaine closely.
Wu eyed the coolie until the new
comer could almost feel the master's
penetrating gaze, although his head
was bowed in awe. Quickly the coolie
thrust his hand under his blouse and
drew forth a package. With another
bow he advanced.
"For your enemies, O master," he
said, handing the package over to
Wu.
The coolie started to open the pack
age, removed the paper wrapper, and
then a silk wrapping inside. Finally
he came to a box, from which he drew
a leather pouch, each operation con
ducted with greater care as it became
evident that the contents were espe
cially precious in some way. Then he
took from the pouch a small vial.
“What is it?" demanded Wu Fang,
as the coolie displayed it.
The coolie draw forth now a magni
fying glass and a glass slide. Opening
the vial with great care he shook
something out on the slide, then
placed it under the lens.
"Look!” he said simply.
Wu bent over and looked. Under
the lens what had formerly seemed to
be merely a black speck of dirt be
came now one of the most weird and
uncanny little creatures to be found in
all the realm of nature. It seemed to
be all legs and feelers moving at once.
A normal person would have looked
at the creature only with the greatest
repugnance. Wu regarded it with a
sort of unholy fascination.
“And it is?” he queried.
“What the white man calls the Afri
can tick which carries the recurrent
fever," answered the coolie deferen
tially.
A flash of intense exultation seemed
to darken Wu Fang's sinister face.
“Wait, ’ he said to the coolie, as he
w
moved slowly again into the back
room.
Long Sin had remained there. With
Weepy Mary he was guarding Elaine,
when Wu Fang re-entered. Elaine
was thoroughly aroused by this time.
“Suffering is a state of mind," Wu
said In a low tone, “and 1 have de
cided that it would be poor revenge
for me to harm you. You are free."
Nothing could have come as a great
er surprise to Elaine.
"Do you—do you really mean it?"
she asked, scarcely able to believe
what her ears heard.
Wu merely nodded, and with a wave
of his hand to Long Sin indicated that
Elaine was to be released.
She nerved herself to take several
steps toward the door. She had
scarcely crossed half the room.
“Wait!" ordered Wu sharply.
Was he merely torturing her, as a
cat might torture a mouse? She
stopped obediently, afraid to look at
him.
"This will be the vengeance of Wu
Fang,” he went on impressively.
"Slowly, one by one, your friends will
weaken and die, then your family, un
til finally only you are left. Then will
come your turn.”
He stopped again and raised his
long, lean forefinger. “Go,” he hissed
'I wish you much joy.”
I don't think that in all our friend
ship I have ever seen Kennedy so ut
terly depressed as he was when we
returned after the discovery of the
vast fortune which Bennett had clev
erly secreted.
There seemed to be absolutely noth
ing to do until some new clue turned
up. I placed my hand on his shoulder,
but the words that would encourage
him died on my lips. Several times I
started to speak, but each time 1
checked myself. There did not seem
to be anything that would be appro
priate for such an occasion.
A sharp ring at the telephone made
both of us fairly jump, so nervous
bad we become. Kennedy reached
aver instantly for the instrument in
the vague hope that at last there was
some news.
As I watched his face it changed
first from despair to wonder, and final
ly it seemed to light up with the most
remarkable look of relief and happi
ness that one could imagine.
“1 shall be right over,” he cried,
jamming the receiver down on the
hook, and in the same motion reach
ing for his hat and coat. “Walter."
he cried, “it is Elaine! They have let
her go!”
I seized my own hat and coat in
time to follow him. and we dashed out
of the laboratory.
The suspense under which Aunt
Josephine had been living had told on
r
He Felt the Edge of the Dagger and
Gazed Significantly at Elaine.
her. Her niece. Elaine's cousin, Mary
Itrown, who lived at Rockledge, had
come into the city to comfort Aunt
Josephine, and they had been sitting
that morning in the library.
Suddenly a startled cry from Jen
nings out in the hall caused both la
dies to jump to their feet. They could
scarcely believe what they heard as
the faithful old butler cried out the
name.
"Why, Miss Elaine'" he gasped.
An instant later Elaine herself burst
into the room and flung herself into
Aunt Josephine's arms. All talking
and half crying from joy at once, they
crowded about her.
Iu the excitement Aunt Josephine
had seized the telephone and called
our number. She did not even wait to
break the good news, but handed the
telephone to Elaine herself.
We left the laboratory on the run,
too fast to notice that just around the
building line at the corner stood a lim
ousine with shades drawn. Even if
we had paused to glance back we
could not have seen Wu Fan* and
I-iong Sin Inside gazing out through
the comer of the curtains. They were
in European dress now, and had evi
dently come prepared for just what
they knew was likely to happen.
We were admitted by the faithful
Jennings and almost ran into the li
brary.
“Oh. Craig'-’ cried Elaine, as Ken
nedy, almost speechless, seized her by
both hands.
For a few seconds none of us could
speak. Then followed a veritable flood
of eager conversation.
"Won't you come and stay with
me a few days up in the country,
dear?-' urged Mary at last.
Elaine thought a moment, then
turned to Aunt Josephine.
“Yes.'' considered her aunt, “I think
it would do you good ”
Still she hesitated, then shyly looked
at Kennedy and laughed. “You, too,
Craig, must be fagged out,” she said,
frankly. “Come up there with us and
take a rest.’’
Kennedy smiled. “I shall he de
lighted," he accepted promptly.
We chatted, then left a few mo
ments later so that Kennedy could
pack.
Around the corner from the labora
tory. as we dashed out. had been, as
I have said, Wu Fang and Long Sin
looking out from the limousine. No
sooner had we disappeared across the
campus than’their driver started up
the car and they sped around to our
apartment.
Cautiously they alighted and walked
down the street. Then, making sure
they were not observed, they entered
and mounted the stairs to our door
way. Long Sin was stationed down
the hall on guard, while Wu Fang drew
from his pocket a blank key, a file and
a candle. He lighted the candle and
held the key in its flame until it was
covered with soot.
Then he inserted the key into the
keyhole, turned it and took the key
out. He filed the key. trying it again
and again. Finally he finished and
opened the door. Reckoning Fong Sin,
he entered our room.
As they stood there Wu Fang gazed
about our living room keenly. He was
evidently considering where to place
something.
Finally his eye rested on the tele
phone It seemed to suggest an idea
to him and he crossed over to it. Care
fully holding down the receiver on the
hook, he unscrewed the case which
holds the diaphragm, while with his
clever fingers he held the rest of the
instrument intact. Then he removed
from his pocket the vial which the
coolie had given him and placed its
contents on the diaphragm itself.
Quickly now he replaced the receiver
and, having finished their work. Long
Sin and Wu Fang stealthily crept out.
A second time, as we approached
our apartment after the visit to Klaine,
we were too excited to notice the
limousine in which were Wu and Long
Sin. Rut no sooner had we entered
than Long Sin left the car with a
final word of instruction from his
master.
Fpstairs, in the apartment, Kennedy
began hurriedly to pack, and 1 helped
him as well as I could. We were in
the midst of it when the telephone
rang and I answered it.
"Hello!" 1 called
There was no response.
“Hello, hello!" 1 repeated, raising
my voice.
Still there was no answer. I worked
the hook up and down, hut could get
no reply, and finally, disgusted, hung
up.
A moment later, I recall now, it
seemed to me as though some one had
stuck a pin into the lobe of my ear.
We had scarcely got back to work
when the telephone bell jangled again,
and a second time I answered it.
“Is Mr. Kennedy there?" came back
1 handed the instrument to Craig.
"Ilelto!" lie called. ‘‘Who is this?”
No response.
"Hello, hello!” he shouted, working
the hook as I had done and, in my
case, there was still no answer.
Neither of us thought anything of
it at the time, but now 1 recall that 1
did see Kennedy once or twice press
the lobe of his ear as though some
thing had hurt it.
We did not know' until later that in
a pay station down the street our
arch enemy, Long Sin, had been call
ing us up and then, with a wicked
smile, refusing to speak to us.
It was about a week later that I
came home late one night from the
Star, feeling pretty done up. What
ever it was, a violent fever seemed
to have come on me suddenly. The
next morning when I woke up I felt
a lassitude that made it quite hard
enough even to lounge about in my
bathrobe Finally, feeling no better,
I decided to see a doctor. I put on
my clothes with a decided effort and
went out.
The nearest doctor was about half
a block away and we scarcely knew
him, for neither Kennedy nor I are
exactly sickly.
He sounded me and pounded me,
talked a little while on generalities,
diet and exercise, then walked over
to a cabinet, and emptied out a few
pills into a little paper box.
“Take one every hour,” he said.
‘‘Cut your cigarettes to three a day,
and don’t drink coffee. Four dollars,
please.’
I paid him and went back to the
apartment, my head soon in a whirl
from a new onset of the fever.
I managed to get back into my bath
robe, and threw myself down on the
divan, propped up with pillows. 1 had
taken the pills, but they had no more
effect than sugar or milk. By this
time I was much more delirious and
was crying out.
I saw faces about me. but I did not
see the faces which were actually out
by our hall door. Wu Fang and Long
Sin had waited patiently for their re
venge. Now that they thought suffi
cient time had elapse#, they had sto
len silently to the apartment door.
While Ling Sin watched. Wu listened.
The w hite devil has it,” whispered
Wu Fang, as he rejoined his fellow
conspirator.
How long I should have remained
in this state, and in fact how long I
did remain, 1 don't know Vaguely, 1
recall that our acquaintance, Johnson,
who had the apartment across the
hall, at last heard my cries and came
in.
Somehow or other I knew the state
I was in I knew it was Johnson, yet
it all seemed unreal to me. With a
great effort I gathered all my scat
tered wits and managed to shout out,
“Telegraph Kennedy—Rockledge.”
•••••••
At about the same time, up at Rock
ledge. Kennedy and Elaine, with her
cousin. Mary lirown, were starting out
for a horseback ride through the hills.
They wjre chatting gayly, but Ken
nedy waf forcing himself to do so.
In fae^ they had scarcely gone half
a mile <hen Kennedy, who was rid
ing between the two and fighting off
by sheer|ierve the illness he felt, sud
denly fel over in half a faint on the
looked inside. Then he looked closer
There was something peculiar about 1
and he picked up a blank sheet o
white paper, dusting off the diphragn
on it. There, on the paper, were in
numerable little black specks.
Just then, outside. Doctor Godow
ski's car drew up and he jumped out
swinging his black bag. Not being ac
quainted with what we were goin(
through, Godowski did not notice th<
almond-eyed Chinaman who was
watching down the street.
“How do you do. doctor?" greetec
Craig faintly, at the door.
“What seems to be the difficulty?'
inquired the doctor eagerly.
“I don't know,” returned Craig
“but 1 have my suspicions. I’m too 11
to verify them myself, so I've called
on you. Look at Jameson first,” he
added.
While Godowski was examining me
Craig managed to get out his micro
scope and was looking through it al
the strange black specks on the pa
per. There, under the lens, he could
see the most remarkable, almost mi
croscopic creature, all legs and feel
ers. a most vicious object.
Weak though he was, he could not
help an exclamation of exultation at
his discovery, just as Godowski had
finished with me.
“Look!” he cried, calling the doctor.
“I know what the trouble is, Godow
ski.”
He had started to tell, but the ex
took!” Said Kennedy, Turning to H?s Microscope.
horse's neck, laine and Mary reined
up their horsey
"Why, Craig,tried Elaine, startled,
‘‘what’s the niter?”
I he sound oher voice seemed to
arouse him. HAraced up. "Oh, noth
ing. 1 guess 1 said with a forced
smile. “I'm all ght.”
It was no useiiowever They had
to cut short the tie, and Kennedy re
turned to the hot glad to drop dow n
m an easy chairln the porch, while
‘-laine hovered aiut him solicitously.
They summonethe country doctor,
but it took him stie time to get out
to the house. Siienly a messenger
boy rode up on hi^icycle and mount
ed the porch stei ‘‘Telegram for
Mr. Kennedy." he Enounced, looking
about and picking W Craig naturally
as the person he t^ted.
Kennedy nodded,ul took the yel
low envelope whileiiaine signed for
it. Listlessly he fe it open. It
read:
Craig Kennedy,
Care Wellington b^vn, '
Rockledge, N. Y.
Jameson very ill. \A,ts you. Better
come. Johnson.
The message seem&0 rouse Ken
nedy in spite of his ^r. ais faee
showed keen alarm. w^|, he endeav
orded to conceal from nine. But ner
quick eye had caught ( look.
"I must see Walter,T. exclaimed,
rising rather weakly f( going into
the house.
How he ever did it is h i think, a
mystery to him. but hManaged to
pack up and, in spite ot iAlternating
fever and chills, makeie journey
back to the city.
When at last Craig aryj at 0ur
apartment, it must hav&emed to
him that he found m^most at
death’s door.
Ill himself, Kennedy thj himself
down for a moment, exhaus^ "When
did this thing come on, \ter?” he
asked of Johnson.
"Yesterday, 1 think, as njy as j
can find out,” replied our frk
Craig was decidedly Worried.
"There's only one person in k York
to call on," he murmured, pi^- him
self out of bed and gettingt0 the
living room as best he could.
‘is that you. Godowski?' lisked
over the telephone. “WellWct0r,
this is Kennedy. Come ovel my
apartment, quick. I've a c^-two
cases, for you.”
Godowski was a world-famoukjen
tist in his line and had special jn
bacteriology, mainly in tropityjg.
eases.
As Kennedy hung up the r^er
ha made his way back again \he
bedroom, scratching his ear. I^0.
ticed that I was doing the satja
my delirium. ,
“Has Walter been scratching ]
ear?" he asked of Johnson.
Johneson nodded. “That's strac j
considered Craig, thoughtfully.
been doing the same.” ]
He turned back into the living r\ *
and for a moment looked about, i •
nally his eye happened to fall on I
telephone and an idea seemed to ' (
cur to him. \t
He went over to the instrument a;
unscrewed the receiver Care
citement of the lourney and the exer
tion were so great that he could hard
ly mumble.
"Here- look—on this paper.’ he
cried. “Prom tho telephone—'
lie had risen and was handing the
paper to the scientist when his weak
ness overcame him. lie fell flat on his
face on the floor and dropped the pa
per spilling the contents.
Godowski, now thoroughly alarmed,
bent over Craig. But the delirium
had overcome Kennedy, too.
t'nable to make any sense out of
Craig's broken wanderings, Godowski
lost no time in taking samples of our
blood.
Then he hurried away to his labora
tory in his car. As he did so, however,
Long Sin leaped into a taxicab which
was waiting, and followed.
*»*»**»
In Godowski’s laboratory, where he
was studying tropical diseases, the
bacteriologist set to work at once to
confirm his own growing suspicions.
From a monkey which he had there
for experimenal purposes, he drew off
some blood samples. Then, with the
aid of his assistant, he took the blood
samples he had obtained from
us. The monkey's blood, under the
microscope, seemed full of rather
elongated, wriggling germs, of a pe
culiar species. In and out they made
their way among the blood corpuscles,
each like a dart aimed at life itself.
Then he took the samples of our
blood. In them were the same germs
carried by that gruesome tick!
"The spirillum! ” he muttered. “They
are infected with African recurrent
fever. The only remedy is atoxyl, ad
ministered intravenously, after the
manner of Professor Ehrlichs famous
'606.' ”
Godowski had rung the call box has
tily for a messenger, when Long Sin.
"ho had managed stealthily to creep
up to the doctor's laboratory window,
scowled through at the action—then
moved away.
While his assistant gathered the ap
paratus, the doctor wrote:
Miss Anne Septix.
301 W. -th St.:
Please go at once to the apartment
of Craig Kennedy, - Claremont
Ave. Surgical case.
GODOWSKI, M. D.
The hoy arrived finally and the doc
or save him a generous tip, to hurry
Kith the note.
He had turned the corner, however,
vhen Long Sin appeared. Subtly he
slaved on the boy's cupidity to get
,lm to deliver a note of his own—
n en offered to deliver the boy's note
or him. The flash of a five dollar bill
nade the rest easy.
As the boy disappeared on a fake
rrand. Long Sin, with the real note,
lurried downtown, smiling wickedly.
"They have discovered the fever,
naster," he reported in the den.
Wu was beside himself with rage
h'fore he could speak, however. Long
on spread .out Godowski's message.
But 1 have this," he added.
It took merely a glance to suggest
o \\ u a new plan of action. He rose
nd moved quickly into the backroom,
t ome, ' he ordered Weepy Mary,
'ou must dress up as a nurse—im
]
i

rr tq as cr ry c
mediately." Quickly sue donned one
of the numerous disguises,
planned his campaign
■ Here," he directed when she
ready, handing her a little via .
must infect every instrument the doc
tor uses on Kennedy and Jameson
BeIhe nodded, and a moment later was
on her way uptowm , . ♦
Meanwhile Godowski himself hac,
arrived at our apartment, much to the
relief of our friend Johnson, and was
unpacking his instruments.
Quickly he improvised two operat
ing tables and placed one of us on
each Then, with his assistant, he put
on his white robe, mask, gloves and
other precautions for asepsis, setting
out the apparatus for the intravenous
administration of the drug that would
bill tho anirilllim.
They had finished their preparations
and were waiting for Miss Septix.
“She ought to be here now,'' muttered
Godowski impatiently, looking at his
watch.
Just then a cab drove up outside.
“Perhaps that is she!” he exclaimed.
“It must be.”
A few moments later the door of
the apartment opened. His face showed
his disappointment. It was a stranger.
"Miss Septix is ill,” she introduced,
“and sent me to take her place.
The doctor looked about. "Very
well, then,” he said briskly, seeing his
preparations. "Are you ready to go
ahead?”
She nodded and threw off the coat
that covered her immaculate white
uniform.
The specialist plunged wholeheart
edly into his work of saving us now.
“Hand me that needle, please.” he
directed the false nurse.
She moved over to the table near
by and took it up, pausing only long
enough to dip it secretly into a vial
she carried with her.
“Please hurry,” repeated the doctor.
She turned from the table and hand- ,
ed it to him. He adjusted it and al
ready held it poised for the thrust
which was not to cure but to poison
us further.
"Weepy Mary!” cried a frightened
voice at our door.
Elaine had been deeply alarmed by
the sudden illness of Kennedy and the
message from Jameson No sooner
had Kennedy gone than it flashed over
her that Wu Fang had predicted some
thing like this.
‘The threat!” she exclaimed, seek
ing her cousin. “Mary, 1 must go to
the city—right away.”
On the next train, then, she had
been speeding back to New York
One glance at the improvised hos
pital was enough to alarm her. Hut
the sight that had transfixed her was
of a woman whose face she remem- !
bered well, though Kennedy and 1 had
never seen her
“Please, Miss,’ began Godowski's !
assistant, trying to quiet Elaine, while
Godowski turned in vexation to his
work.
"No, no!" repeated Elaine. “This
woman is no nurse. She is a crim
inal!"
Godowski paused. It was true he
did not know the woman. He gazed
from Elaine to Weepy Mary in doubt.
The game was up. Weepy Mary
dropped a piece of gauze which she
had soaked in the solution from the
vial which Wu had given her and bolt
ed for the door.
So sudden was her flight that no
one was quick enougti to stop her
i ne exc itement, more than ever,
alarmed Klaine now. "Tell me.” she ap
pealed to Doctor Godowski, “what is
the matter?”
“In some way,” he replied quickly,
"they have become infected with the
bite of an African tick which carries
spirillum fever.”
Godowski raised his hands in de
spair "I was just about to start," he
cried. "Everything is ready. I can't
send for another nurse. Every min
ute counts."
Elaine had thrown off her ceat and
bat. Her sleeves were up in a mo
ment, and before the doctor knew what
sbe was about she was scrubbing her
bands in the antiseptic wash.
"Only—show me—what to do," she
-rled. "I will be the nurse!"
Several days later, when he had re- !
covered sufficiently from the diaboli
cal attack that had been made upon
ts, Kennedy was again at work in I
-be laboratory, while I was writing.
Our speaking tube sounded, and I
mew that it was Elaine and Aunt Jo
ephine.
"How do you feel?” inquired Elaine !
nxiously, as she almost ran across
he laboratory to Craig
"Fine!” he exaggerated, brightly.
Really? she repeated anxiously.
“Look!” he said, turning to his mi
roscope.
He turned some blood from a test
ube in our electric incubator and
laced a drop on a slide. It was some
f the blood infected by the germs car
led by the tick.
That is how our blood looked—be
>re the new nurse arrived,” he smiled,
hile Elaine looked at it in horror
Then he pricked his arm and let a
rop smear on another slide.
“Now look at that—perfectly nor- [
al,” he added.
"Oh, I'm so glad,” she exclaimed ra
antly.
“Normal—thanks to you. You saved
3. You were just in time,” cried Craig,
iking both her hands in his.
He was about to kiss her, when she
roke away. "Craig,” she whispered,
lushing and looking hastily at us.
Aunt Josephine and I could only |
nile at the disgusted glance Craig
ive us, as he thrust his hands in
is pockets and wished us a thousand
lies away at that moment.

soon have to look for a substitute for
high-priced wheat bread. We have
the advantage of Europe in possess

the high price of wheat will doubt
less mean the spread of the popular
ity of corn bread, from its native
southland, throughout the country.
Mrs. Burleson, wife of the postmaster
general, sees nothing to be dreaded in
this, and gives the recipe for the "corn
dodger,” one of her favorites, which j
she declares to be more healthful than
white bread. “Measure and scald the

just enough boiling water over the

and mix the batter with enough cold
water to spread a little or mold it
in small dodgers in the hands. This
should be baked in the oven on a well
greased griddle, or flattened into
cakes and cooked on top of the stove
| until a rich brown. With butter ther
: is nothing better." Southern won
en contend that sugar, eggs and oth«
expensive ingredients are not nece
sary to make good corn bread. The
ought to know, as they have mad
corn bread to perfection iu the Sout
for many generations.

Don't forgot to (lose the rofrigera

Kumiss.
This dish is of great value in the

Sift together two cupfuls of flour,

Soak a pound of codfish six hours in

\\ tyn cold, pick into (lakes with a fork

Is yet nicer if you add a raw egg be

Prepare tomatoes as In the preced
ng recipe, omitting the sauce. Keep

Arrange the tomatoes on a dish, lay a

If for any reason it is not conveni

iry and served in the same fashion.
Sausage Bundles.
Roll out plain paste In six-inch

n the center of the piece of paste;
Jouble, pinch ends together and fold
is you would do up a bundle, wetting
:he edges to made them stick; then

Hake in hot oven 15 minutes and

Soap Economy.
Soap should be stored some time be
ore using in order to get the best
talue out of it. Remove wrapper, put
he soap on a tin and place in a hot
>ven until moist, but be careful not to

ihape. Remove and place in a cool
iput. In a few hours it will be found
lard and will last twice as long.

SYNOPSIS.
f The New York police are mystified by a
aeries of murders and other crimes. The
principal clue to the criminal la the warn
ing letter which Is sent the victims, signed
’with a “clutching hand.” The latest vic
tim of the mysterious assassin is Taylor
jDodge. the Insurance president. His
■daughter, Elaine, employs Craig Kennedy,
'the famous scientific detective, to try to
unravel the mystery. What Kennedy ac
complishes Is told by his friend Jameson,
a newspaper man. After many fruitless
attempts to put Elaine and Craig Ken
nedy out of the way the Clutching Hand
is at last found to be none other than
Perry Bennett. Elaine’s lawyer and the
;man she is engaged to marry. Bennett
flees to the den of one of his Chinese
criminals. The Chinaman forces from
•Bennett the secret of the whereabouts of
*7.0u0,W0. Then he gives the lawyer a
•potion which will suspend animation for
months. Kennedy reaches Bennett's side
just after he has lost consciousness.
EIGHTEENTH EPISODE
THE VENGEANCE OF WU FANG.
Elaine was still in the power of Wu
Fang. Kennedy had thwarted the

Slowly he drew from under his robe

Just then Long Sin entered. “One

Wu understood. It had been a pleas
ant fiction of his that although he did

Out In the front room, just a mo

He bowed as Long sin tacea nun.

So, almost by chance, Wu was inter

The coolie started to open the pack
age, removed the paper wrapper, and

The coolie draw forth now a magni
fying glass and a glass slide. Opening

"Look!” he said simply.
Wu bent over and looked. Under
the lens what had formerly seemed to
be merely a black speck of dirt be
came now one of the most weird and
uncanny little creatures to be found in

A normal person would have looked

"And it is?” he queried.
"What the white man calls the Afri
can tick which carries the recurrent
fever,” answered the coolie deferen
tially.
A flash of Intense exultation seemed
*o darken Wu Fang's sinister face.
"Wait,” he said to the coolie, as he

It was about a week later that I

The nearest doctor was about half

He sounded me and pounded me,

‘‘Take one every hour,” he said.

I paid him and went back to the

I managed to get back into my bath

I saw faces about me, but I did not

PRAISES THE “CORN DODGER”
Wife of Postmaster-General Gives
Recipe for Food Which Sh#
Declares Delicious.
In the interests of economy and con
serving the food supply, the German
housewife has been told to mix rye
and potato flour with wheat in mak
ing bread, remarks Frances Frear in
Leslie’s. In view of the excessive
amount of wheat the United States
has exported to the warring nations,
and the consequent rise in the price
of flour, the American housewife may
soon have to look for a substitute for
high-priced wheat bread. We have
the advantage of Europe in possess
ing another great staple—corn, and
the high price of wheat will doubt
less mean the spread of the popular
ity of corn bread, from Its native
southland, throughout the country.
Mrs. Burleson, wife of the postmaster
general, sees nothing to be dreaded in
this, and gives the recipe for the “corn
dodger," one of her favorites, which
she declares to be more healthful than
white bread. “Measure and scald the
meal,” says Mrs. Burleson. “Pour
just enough boiling water over the
meal to moisten it. Add salt accord
ing to the quantity of cornmeai used,
then a tablespoonful or two of cot
tonseed oil—I always use the oil—
and mix the batter with enough cold
water to spread a little or mold it
in small dodgers in the hands. This
should be baked in the oven on a well
greased griddle, or flattened into
cakes and cooked on top of the stove
until a rich brown. With butter there
ia ' nothing better." Southern wom
en contend that sugar, eggs and other
expensive ingredients are not neces
sary to make good corn bread. Th£y
ought to know, as they have made
corn bread to perfection in the South
for many generations.
Geologists Needed in Armies.
Since war has left the realm of fiery
romance and become a matter of dig
ging the enemy out, of lead trenches,
approach trenches, sapping, under
ground barracks and other evolutions
of the spade and pickax, new problems
face the general staff. It is becoming
gradually evident that to the various
branches of knowledge that go to
make up the science of strategy there
must be added another—namely, ge
ology. It is not enough to be able to
pick out a hillside or bit of meadow
j land that will best lend itself to the
tactics planned. The commanding offi
cer, fighting now a war of intrench
: ments, must choose a position that
will also be diggable. If beneath a de
ceptive light surface there is nothing
but rock, trenches will be impossible,
and his troops will have no chance
against an intrenched enemy.—Lit
erary Digest.
Eternal Curiosity.
So you think all public entertain
ments should be censored.”
"Yes And I’d like to be one of the
censors.”
"What for?”
“So as to be sure of not missing
anything.”
RIGHTS OF THE DEFEATED
Law Journal Comments on Restric
tions Placed on Lawyer Who
Has Lost a Case.
The Central Law Journal says that
It has been thought by a good many
that the losing lawer has something
of an immemorial right to go outside
and "cuss” the court, and now comes
along the Kentucky court of appeals
and holds that he actually has such a
right in regard to a jury which haa
rendered a verdict against his client
(Smallwood vs. York, 173 S. \V. 380.)
All of this, however, is to be taken
with a grain of allowance, for he must
not attribute to the jury “the commis
sion of a crime involving moral turpi
tude for which the ‘jury’ might be in
dicted or punished." This leaves, nev
ertheless, large latitude in explosive
utterances for the relief of a mind
surcharged with grief and commend
able anxiety to explain the defeat of
an attorney to his once hopeful client.
While the court let the attorney oft
in the above case; in which one ot the
jury objected to his vitriolic elo
quence, employed after verdict, we
are somew'hat grieved to see it de
ploring the use by the attorney of this
safety valve by calling it “highly im
proper.” Pretty soon we may expect
that such a court will be announcins
that there ought to be some restric
tion on practitioners in Kentucky
when they may wish to submit some
oral or written remarks in free and
liberal criticism—and which should
not be printed—of itself. Must the
loser find no balm in Gilead for his
wounded spirit?
Soldiers’ Bad Dreams.
Still another exasperating and par
ticularly horrible nightmare was the
discovery of a live shell in the bed,
on the point of exploding, and from
which the dreamer was unable ’to es
cape. Several men dreamed they
were on the point of being bayoneted
themselves because they could not
withdraw their own bayonets from the
body of a man they had just killed.
Sudden awakenings caused by the i
nearby explosion of shells often pro-'
duced curious semihysterical condi
tions in the more nervous patients.
In one case there was a partial blind
ness; the man could not connect let-!
ters into words; he could read the let
ters, but could not pronounce the
word when spelled, nor write it when
pronounced.—New York Evening Post.
Baseball Vs. Art.
I was looking over an unusually
good collection of pictures in an all
around sporting room in Atlanta, Ga.,
and had just stopped before a familiar
sketch of Charles Dana Gibson, “Three
Strikes and the Bases Full," when I
noticed two men, who had evidently
studied more baseball than art, gaz
ing at the same picture. Suddenly
one of them burst out: “Ain't that
good? Look at them faces." After a
further examination he said: "I never
would have knowed it, but it's by Gib
son: he’s the best catcher on the
Pittsburgh team. I didn’t know he
could draw.”—I os Amgeles Times

Cabin and Paint Creeks Miners Expect

Paint creek and Cabin creek, as a part

ties and rails.”
