fans turn In at the ends. Some are round
In shape and many are hand-painted and
embroidered.
One use of the fan Is to shade the eyes
from the eun. The most fashionable gen
tleman's cap has no brim and the man who
has no fan cannot walk about without an
umbrella. Many of the people go bare
headed, and, as they are shaved to the
crown, they must have something to pro
tect them from the hot sun. In the
shade the fan Is either carried In the hand
or folded up and stuck in the back of the
neck or sometimes In the top of the stock
ing. Hangchow has the largest fan store of the
world. It face the main street, and con
sists of many rooms running around courts
and covering a large space. In the front
part of the establishment a dozen clerks are
kept busy showing fans to would-be pur
chasers. The other rooms are workshops,
where Chineae men, women and children
are making fans. In this store the busl
neas 1 done In department, on set of
men making the sticks, another painting
the leaves and others making th nails
which Join the sticks together. In most
businesses on Chinese make th whole
product, but the fan business her 1 or
ganized Into many division.
They Pal at aad Powder.
Among th other queer shops of Hang
chow ar those which sell perfumery and
cosmetics. The Chinese women paint and
powder more than their sister of the
United Statea. The women cover their
cheek with rouge. They shav their eye
brow and pencil out new one after th
most fashionable arch. They paint the
lips red and paate the neck over with
ghastly white. The painting 1 don by
femalea of all ages, from little girls to old
women.
Other odd shops are the silversmiths,
where most beaut. ful cupa, pitchers and
other thing ar sold. Among the curious
articles are silver shields to protect the
long fingernails of fashionable women.
Both sexes allow the nails to grow and I
have seen men who could put the palm of
one hand on their chin and scratch the
back of the neck without moving it. Long
nails, you know, ar th signs of a gentle
man, tor no one who has nails two or three
Inches long can possibly do monlal labor.
I am surprised at the good order of the
Interior Chinese cities. Th people are
courteous and ery polite. In Hangchow I
am treated everywhere with drfirence, and,
though a crowd follows me whm I attempt
to take pictures, no stoues are thrown, nor.
aa far as I can learn, are any rude things
said. We have cow grown accustomed to
the Chinese, but twenty years ago a party
of celestials In gowns would have been
mobbed In many of our Interior towns, and
the boys would probably have thrown
atone at them. Certainly a Chinese woman
dressed aa ah 1 at horn could not go
about upon her small feet In our rltle to
day without a crowd of boys at her heels.
The street here are so crowded that It
would be Impossible to do business if the
people were not very good-natured. Very
tew policemen are required. In the four-

CANTON The Chines? boycott
against American goods will cost the
business interests of the United States
millions of dollars. The embargo
against Yankee products has gone In
to effect in seventeen of the eighteen
provinces of the iloivery kingdom, and
even if immediate steps are taken to
stop the agitation against American
wares, irreparable loss has already
been done to our commerce. It will
take many months, perhaps years, to
recover the ground we have lost in
this great market. This is all the
more to be regretted because it might
have been prevented. The causes
leading up to this extensive boycott
all revert primarily to the work of
bungling or corrupt American officials.
The American merchants held a de
cided advantage in the vast market of
China, because our government has
always befriended the Celestial Em
pire. Our influence has been exerted
to preserve the integrity of China
when Europe clamored for its parti
tion. We are the only power that has
resisted the temptation to take a slice
fot its rich territory when others were

OUR TREATY "WITH CHINA. ! i
. The trouble reverts to official abuse V
of the treaty of 1880 regulating Chi- ;
nese immigration to the United States, j i
the second article of which reads: i V
''Chinese subjects, whether proceeding j
to the United States as teachars, stu- j i
dents, merchants, or from curiosity, : Sr
together with their body and house
hold servants, and Chinese laborers
who are now in the United States,
shall be allowed to go and come of
their own free will and accord, and
shall be accorded all the rights, privi
leges, immunities and exemptions which
are accorded to the citizens and sub
jects of the most favored nations."
This ruling is so plain that, there can
be no mistaking the rights of certain
Chinese subjects named therein, yet
official action in the United States has
become so strict that for years no
Chinese, no matter how important a
personage he might be, could enter our
country without being subjected to a
most sweeping and embarrassing ex
amination. This state of affairs re
sulted first because both native and
American officials in China committed
frauds by giving merchant and student
certificates to coolie?!, and because
the western officials (particularly
those of San Francisco) have been
, over-zealous in catering to the labor
vote of their State. In their determi
nation to keep out the coolie, who is
not wanted and who cannot enter le
, gaily, "they have brought about a more
;'verious consequence by offending and
arraying against us ihe best class of
Chinese. They have gone from one
extreme to the other. Thousands of
coolies in America could not have
( damaged us as much as this boycott
' will.
CHINESE NOT TREATED FAIRLY.
Many specific instances are cited
where prominent Chinese have been
treated with flagrant disrespect and
often refused admittance to the coun
try. Not long ago a young student
iwas sent back because his papers did
not state what .school in the Ur.ited
States he proposed to attend. He had
the regular consular certificate, a let
ter from an American missionary
stating that the Chinese Consul in
America should determine which col
lege .it would be be?t for the young
man to attend, and other letters be
sides; yet the immigration officers held
that his papers were irregular because
ho flirt not know which school he

TROUBLE BREWING FOR TEARS.
This sort of thing has been going on
for years. The better clacs of Chinese
have repeatedly remonstrated, but our
officials have either been indifferent to
fheir complaints, or have taken only
weak-kneed measures to rectify the
abuses. The excuse has always been
that coolies were appearing on our
borders with improper papers secured
in China. There is no doubt but that
American officials have been responsi
ble for much fraudulent practice in
this respect, but because we have had
unscrupulous men as our representa
tives in this part of the world it does
not follow that we should insult every
Chinese that comes our way.
If we had had the right men in China
in years pst. this boycott would not
have occurred. They would have
kric.M. that the growi feeling of in
dignation among the better element of
Chinese would culminate in some such
measure of retaliation, and they would
have put the matter up to Washington
in such a manner that the abuses
would have been corrected before it
was too late. At it is they have either
abetted the evil or have been indif
ferent to it, and now we are in for it.
PLAN OF THE BOYCOTT.
The boycott originated in Shanghai,
and the following bulletin outlining
the plan of procedure was issued by
the general committee and sent broad
cast over the Empire:
(1) To compile all cases in which
Chinese have been ill-treatel by
Americans, either in the United States,
Cuba or the Philippine Islands.
(2) To engage men in the interior
to spread the plans of the system of
boycotting and to post bills regarding i
the same on wall's so as to make it uni
versally known to the public.

the public warned against them." All
such will be treated as beasts, and no
funeral or matrimonial ceremony will
be performed for them.
WAGING THE CAMPAIGN.
The above bulletin was followed by
five kinds of posters, all directing the
public to beware of American prod
ucts. One of these was a very elab
orate bill enumerating the kinds of
American goods on sale in China, ac
companied by a list of their trade
mark". This poster was so ingenious
that it was suspected some of our
European competitors had something
to do with it. To thoroughly spread
the plan of the boycott and acquaint
the public with its purpose, the actors
in all theaters were hired to receive
doggerel versos, and even singing
girls on the streets were given songs
referring to the boycott. All priests
were instructed to thoroughly post
their followers not to have anything
to do with Americans or their goods.
Even the doctors were ordered not to
use American drugs or instruments in
their practice. The newspapers flatly
refused American advertisements, and
none of the Chinese publications would
print anything favorable to the Ameri
can cause.
The mind of the Chinese coolie is
very simple, and peculiar measures
are necessary to reach his comprehen

natives not to ride on the street cars
in Hongkong because they were made
in America. It consisted of a crude
drawing of a street1 car, the seats of
Which were occupied by lizards,
snakes, dogs, cats and frogs. The
reading accompanying the illustration
explained that all persons who rode
on these cars would likely be turned

far. His sins against the Govern
ment of India were many and varied
Under his stimulating influence, ?
petty boundary dispute would de
velop, first into an affair with swords
and ultimately into a tribal war
Among his followers were the an
archists of the hills, men who livec
by plunder, intrigue, and bloodshed
When the fancy texile them, thej
raided peaceful territory, reveled ii

turned to their stronghold in broae

Political Agent who had sworn to love and honor therr
if they obeyed the British Raj?and paid revenue anc
cattle ta:;. Thereupon, that hardworked servant ol
the Empire would lift up his voice and curse Pii
Mahomed, and importune his Government to carry out
the promises he had made in its name. But Calcutta
and Simla are a long way from the northwest frontier
and the voice of the Political Agent seldom reaches sc
far, especially if he be merely a civilian.
It was when the works of a new cantonment had beer
begun on the lands of the Utman Khels that Pir Ma
homed erred too greatlv. In a spirit of lightheartec
frolic he sent down a band of mixed outlaws to the camp
at night. In cold blood they murdered seven coolies anc
vanished almost before the alarm reached the military
side. It is one thing to harass a Political Agent nearlj
to death, and quite another to snap the fingers of scorr
at any part of the British army. Thus was the cup
of Pir Mahomed's iniquity filled to overflowing, and th<
Government of India at last sanctioned the punitive ex
pedition for which the Political Agent had been pleadinp
year i i and year out.
'T'HE force made its appearance in the hill country
early in October, and was so composed as to marl
the gravity of its mission, and to recognize the powei
and influence of Pir Mahomed and the blackness of his
misueeus. i nere was a, uenerai 111 coiiiuiaiiu, auiuc iuuj
thousand infantry with an Irish regiment among them
five hundred cavalry, mountain guns, and sappers anc
miners. Also there were tribal levies galore, and sundry
Sirdars who were out to see the business done anc
done well.
The Sirdar Pir Mahomed fully appreciated the honoi
done him; but he judged it wiser to be away from home
when the men in khaki began to wind al>out the passes
and to climb the bare brown hills. His fort, a stately
and substantial building with four square towers, was
found to be deserted, and so was his adjoining village,
Empty were his granaries also; but here the sepoys
were on familiar ground, and soon one hiding place
after another yielded up ample stores of grain. The
force marched on again, leaving as an army of occupation
a small detachment under a youthful and
joyous subaltern who was prepared for literally anything.
It was then that Pir Mahomed and his people began
to get in their fine work. They sniped at that expedition
i i i j i i r_ii i j.1 : j.
Dy nignt ana Dy uay, ana goou men ieu uy me waysiue
in increasing numbers, picked off by an enemy they had
never seen. Those in command grew very bitter, with
that bitterness which can be endured only in silence.
Death would come swiftly and unerringly from behind
some little boulder on a wide plain, or drop, like a hawk
upon its prey, from the inaccessible heights above.
There was no chance of retaliation?yet. It was a onesided
warfare which might well sicken the bravest heart.
Presently there was a trap in the making, and the
General had word of it. He knew that on the other side
of Amir Pass he would come up with the enemy; but the
pass had to be negotiated lirst. It was nearly four miles
in length, a cleft from head to foot of the mountains that
towered into the blue, a cleft so narrow that a camel's
load would touch the walls of it on each side. Gaping
chasms ran far backward from the brink of the cliffs
above; so that it was impossible to send flanking parties
along up there to protect the main body as it trickled
through below. The force that was found within its
confines was doomed to nothing less than annihilation.
rPHE General moved quickly, and moved unexpectedly,
on a cold, clear morning, very early. The
Pioneers and the Irish, with the Mountain Battery,
went smartly through, and secured the exit?just in
time; for even as they took up their position there apgroached
large bands of armed men. Not followers of
ir Mahomed, no, nor yet sympathizers of his. As suppliants
before God, they affirmed that they had come
to help the soldiers of the British Raj. Nevertheless,
they were unable to conceal some natural disappointment
at not having arrived earlier.
Midway through the pass was a procession of dooly
bearers, and in the doolies were elevtai wounded men.
They went at their ease, understanding that now the
way was secure. In the rear, toiling over the stones and
glacier debris, streamed camp followers, mules, camels,
sheep, and goats, with their scattered escort. Hidden

instantly carried off their feet by the seething crowd
around them. The hillmen, drunk with triumph and
the lust of blood, hacked and hewed their way among
the struggling masses.
The padre stood like a rock in midstream when the
yellow flood comes raving down, bearing upon its
breast flotsam to hurl upon whatever bars its course.
The doctor, caught in the living torrent, was whirled
away to the rear, the doo'y bearers had dropped their
load, and vanished; and still the padre stood, and his
clasp of the dying man's hand was close and warm.
Twenty yards away a giant Pathan, hawk eyed and
srarling with the sheer madness of battle, was plunging
toward the dooly through the slaughtered sheep and
fallen pack animals. The hand that held to the padre
so feeblv relaxed, and Private Colquhoun went home.
Ten yards away, and the Pathan reached a cowering
coolie driver. Him he caught by the head; there was a
thrust of the long knife, a scream that rang above the
screams of fear, and the coolie dropped into the welter
of dead and dying.
"THERE was a cluster of frothing maniacs plowing a
red path after their leader now. The padre saw the
coolie die, and his face hardened. The iron shod pole
of the broken dooly lay at his feet, and he picked it
up. He, who had seemed an easy victim, did not wait
for the onslaught. He met the oncoming charge with
as wild a leap as ever he had taken when he was champion
high jumper at dear, old Alma Mater on College
Green. The Pathan went flying with a dent upon his
skull that would have half killed an ordinary man, and
>? - ? . ? . % i r
tne paare went staggering aown among tne rucK or
animals. A moment, and he was up again, making a
stand with his back against the opposite wall. He
struck out right and left, and the enemy ringed round

Straightening the river will shorten
It ftwn 154 miles between Port Quit

before they can become effective.

Emigration to Straits Settlements
Is Cut 80 Per Cent.
AMOY, China. August 7 UP).— British
consular authorities today instructed
shipping companies transporting coolies
to the Straits Settlements to reduce the
emigration by 80 per cent, or 100,000
annually.
The low price of rubber and tin caused

supply the settlements with most of
their coolie labor, the restriction may
greatly affect prosperity here.

PUT ON RETIRED LIST

Brig. Gen. Herbert O. Williams, for*
mer commander of the 16th Brigade of
the 3d Corps Area, with headquarters
» .

has been transferred to the retired Hat
by operation of law pa account of age.

eral in September, 1926.

text:
Willow Ware
Continued from pat« fix
“I but went to present my poor
gift.”
"Was it for this thou wast flogged
through the streets like a common
thief?”
Ho Nan hung his head. “My friend,
it is not meet to speak of these things.
But that thou may’st completely
understand how great was my desire,
I shall tell thee all.
"Dost know the beauty of Chen
T'si’s garden in this — the mating
season? 1 stood upon the marble
bridge across the tiny lake. The soft
call of a thrush was answered from the
thicket by the willow tree. Tuberoses
and narcissi poured heavy perfume
upon the air. Flaming lilies wantonly
tossed their golden pollen upon the
tranquil bosom of the pool, blending
their reflection with that of the sunset
sky and with the drooping wisteria
bending to kiss the lotus buds. What
wonder that I, seeing Lee Tah curled
upon a mossy bank, felt the same urge
of spring in my heart.”
Wen Chu looked nervously behind
him. "The All-Wise alone knows how
I fear for thy life, Ho Nan. The hon
orable Wu T’sa likes not interference
in his plans, and thou hast already
been beaten like a common thief for
thy presumption. But in famine time,
when our whole village was without
food, thy grandfather came to mine
with fat bags of rice. His good deed is
recorded in the heart of the Budda,
and I will help thee. Ho Nan, for the
Wen family forgets not its friends.”
When four more days of intolerable
agony were ended, the coolies with
drew for the last time the caress of the
dripping whip and smiled with callous
joy at the lacerated back of their
victim. Ho Nan feebly looked up at
the strip of cobalt blue starred above
the compound like a jeweled ribbon.
"It is like a strip of sapphire satin
to bind her brow," he thought weakly
before he fainted.
The gutteral voice of a rickshaw
coolie roused him.
"1 cannot take him,” it was saying.
"His blood will stain my cushions,
which I have just had recovered.”
The clink of copper cash sounded,
then Ho Nan felt a soft cloth tunic
slipped over his head and together
Wen Chu and the now willing coolie
carried him out to the waiting rick
shaw. thence by a circuitous route to
the little thatched house of a friend.
All the soothing remedies of the
medicine man were used, and ere dawn
traced her bright fingers across the
sky. Ho Nan knew for the first time
in many nights the kingdom of dream
less sleep.
ways pass™ on leaaen leei, wnue
the mother of Wen Chu annointed Ho
Nan's wounds with a salve of beetles’
wings and the eyes of black bats, or
prepared him nourishing food. But
one droning evening as the sun de
scended the western slope, a small
scroll of red paper fell into the window
of the room. Ho Nan leaped from the
brick "kang,’* but when he had
reached the tiny grating, only a ser
vant's blue tunic was disappearing
around the comer. Yet he pressed the
bit of paper to his cheek before he read:
"Where Six Harmonies pagoda
Curls its comers heavenward
And a brightly laquered pailow
Arches o’er the gateway high,
Mourns beneath a guarded roof-tree
The pate jewel of thy heart.”
Ho Nan forgot his scarce-healed
back and its benumbing ache. New
power flowed into his veins, awakening
his dormant desire.
Wen Chu was a maxed to find his
friend fully clothed and waiting for
him when he returned from the gold
smith’s shop.
"She has written!” Ho Nan began,
in ill-disguised eagerness. "Upon a red
paper she has told me where she waits.
It is the home of her cousin beyond
the second bridge.”
Wen Chu was aghast that any
maiden should be so immodest as to
write a note to a man. “Thou must,
Ho Nan, be cautious,” he admonished.
"Mayhap it is Wu T’sa who has
written thee, to get thee into another
trap.”
"Do I not know the beautiful char
acters traced by that ivory hand?”
scoffed Ho Nan. "There are no others,
even among the Mandarins, so beau
tiful to read.”
The sleepy gateman drowsing in the
sun scarcely glanced at a stooping
pedler who strode by him the next
morning, but the houseboy eyed the
intruder suspiciously before opening
the swinging door.
“It is early for pedlers,” he re
marked.
Ho Nan smiled ingratiatingly.
"Wilt thou tell thy most gracious
Tai-tai that here is a wanderer from
the Annam country bringing such em
broideries as never were seen, and
asking but a pale jewel in exchanger’
The house boy was amused at these
fine words, and departed with the
message. Ho Nan was immediately
conducted into the women’s court
yard. In spite of the tumultuous beat
ing of his heart, he bent to the floor
before the two women who received
him. spreading at their feet a wealth
of embroidered wonders.
Although Lee Tah seemed to exam
ine his wares, her eyes held a look of
tenderness which was for him alone.
’’Little Lotus Bud,” he whispered,
under cover of a flaming satin pea
cock, “1 have come to thee!" And
from the loose fold of his tunic he
drew forth the jade parrakeet.
A deep look passed between them,
then the other maiden, reaching forth
her hand to examine the cage, laughed
softly. ”0 Peddler, methinks thy bird
is lonely. Perchance she sigheth for
her mate!” Then jumping up, she ran
to the goldfish pool at the end of the
courtyard, out of earshot.
Lee Tab’s pale cheeks were a soft
rose as she bent over the plaques be
fore her, and her thoughts were not of
needlework, nor her eyes upon the ex
quisite peacock, lifelike upon its
background of satin.
“Could’st thou leave thy life of
luxury, Lee Tah; leave thy jewels
and thy servants and do what no girl
has ever done before — come away
with the lover of thy heart!” Ho Nan
was whispering low.
Lee Tah’s perfect almond eyes were
shining, but she did not speak. A
thousand,years of inhibitions numbed
her tongue.
’’He has naught to offer but the toil
of his hands and the worship in his
heart,” begged her lover.
“That is more precious than silver
or jade, Awakener of Dreams,” whis
pered the girl softly.
Then Ho Nan spoke quickly, folding
his silks as he warily watched the
doors.
"We must plan at once then, for I
every second here is hazardous. At j
dawn a foreign boat leaves the river :
for a land far across the Yellow Water.
Once aboard it we shall be safe, ;
for its people, though barbarians, are !
kind of heart. Wilt thou care to come '
with me, Sweet Moon of Evening?”
“It is like the ancient stories of j
the Gods!” replied Lee Tah, her hand 1
clutching her heart.
At evening in the servants’ quarter, !
the house servants were assembled in |
a cross-legged, chattering group.
"Where is Lee Tah, she who was
sent here for punishment?” asked the
Number One Boy, who was a person
of importance.
“She gazed too long at embroi
deries.” answered Lee Tab’s amah. "I
have just embarked her upon the
sampan of sleep.”
Yet her frightened mistress heard
both question and answer as, clad in
the amah’s tunic, she slipped past a
snoring gateman into the street of
the Six Harmonies Pagoda. Under its
curling eaves eager hands met, eager
lips whispered hurried words of love.
Then, fearful of ever present danger,
they fled hand in hand to where a
cabined sampan swayed in the shadow
of the river bank. The tall coolies
uttered a low grunt as they sprang
aboard and pushed out noiselessly into
the stream. Lee Tah sank quivering
to the floor of the tiny cabin.
"We are safe!” she sobbed exul
tantly.
Ho Nan did not answer. Whose had
been those hurrying footsteps behind
him in the quiet of the narrow street?
Well did he know that were they dis
covered, death would be his fate, and
far worse than death, hers; that a
thousand watching spies might have
observed their flight. He watched his
coolie pole his way in and out among
the smaller river craft. The night
seemed full of ominous peril, strange
sounds, chattering voices. After an
eternity of time they reached the
swift currents of the open river, and he
breathed a deep sigh of relief.
Wu T’sa was gorging himself at an
official dinner. Bowl after bowl of
tempting viands vanished in an as
tounding manner, accompanied by the
smacks that signified he was enjoying
his food. For several hours he had vied
with his friends in the important mat
ter of eating and drinking. Now, hav
ing disposed of shark’s fins, litchi nuts
in honey, and that rare delicacy, eggs
one hundred years old. he was musing,
mellow of mood, between swallows of
samshu, upon the kindness of life.
He had reached the pinnacle of his
career, a power in the province, and
now he was about to possess the
beautiful Lee Tah. His small eyes
narrowed as he gloated over her slim
loveliness. He rubbed hia lat hands
together thinking of her satin skin. In
the shop of Po, the pawnbroker, was a
jade and pearl headdress which should
become her well.
A houseboy in a strange tunic dis
turbed his pleasant reveries. Wu T’sa
did not linger over his wine. Roar
ing like a thwarted water buffalo, he
parted from his host in a most un
seemly manner, quite neglecting those
leisurely formalities of adieu which
especially mark the true gentleman of
China.
Far beyond the twinkling lights of
the town with its high, dark walls,
beyond the slow sailing junks and
river crafts, out into the open river
stretching off into the flat distance
like an inert silver serpent. Ho Nan's
matting-rooted sampan slid along.
Camphor trees, bamboos and willows
flanked the river bank, their long
drooping branches screening the mys
tery of the shore. The river, tortuous,
winding, revealed but a few hundred
yards before or behind the small boat.
The night was dark, quiet, danger
ously still.
Suddenly from behind them a
brightly lanterned barge swung into

Washington. October 29. The
.President recently suspended from
office W. A. Stone, District At
torney f.r the Western District o!
Pennsylvania, and 31. II Denton
I'iMrici Attorney for the t estern
District of Missouri. Stone is
ican and Benlon is a Demo
rat, and they were both suspended
f-r the atue cause violation of
t!ie President's order forbidding
Federal officials to interfere in poli
tic. Mr. Stoae was re-appointed
by President Arthur only a short
time before the expiration of his
term, and the Democrats of West
ern Pennsylvania, for nearly two
rears h ire been laboring to secure
his removal. Mr. Beuton was re"
commended for the appointment by
he entire Missouri delegation in
Concress. and his friends think the
-auspension will only be temporary.
Ia Stone's case it is doubtful if he
will ever, be re-appointed. The
Presidents explanation of bis
causes for suspending these officers
is entirely in harmony with his
previous declaration regarding the
propriety of interference of Federal
officers in polities It is probably
a reminder to office holders through
out the country that when the
order was issued it was meant to be
observed. Stone bas been making
speeches in Pennsylvania for
Dearer, the Republican candidate
for Governor, and Benton has taken
the stump for the Democratic ticket
in Missouri. Both men meet an
equal fate and the President ob
tains credit for baing true to his I
promise. The President indorsed
the paper setting forth the facts,
'Let the officer be suspended at
once," and returned it to the At.
torney General for an enforcement
of the order.

INDIANS DETAINED
DY LOCAL POLICE
Held as Jail Breakers Fending
Identification But Later
Released.
Two Sioux Indians from the Stand­
ing Rock reservation were detained
for several hours here early yesterday
morning when a request was received
by the local police officials to be on
the lookout for a pair who had broken
& jail at Fort Yates. The indiacus
could speak a little English and
launched a vigorous protest against
their detention here' with the result,
than an effort was made to have tliera
identified and the discovery made that
they were not the guilty parties. Ac­
cording to the Indian agent who noti­
fied the local authorities of the dis­
appearance of the two Indians at Ft.
Yates, the pair wanted there are said
to be bad actors and notice has been
sent broadcast in hopes of affecting
their capture.

Great Northern yard master, for whom
he expected to go to work today. An
engine nearby was blowing off steij*i.

stepped by to let it pass but as he did

FAMILYMMPPY
DY RECONCILIATION
Wife and Children of Herman C.
Miller pf Ryder Again
Return Home.
Ryder, N. D., Jan. 8.—It was a hap­
py holiday season in the home of
Herman C. Miiler, the family being
again united, aftef domestic difficul­
ties, in which it is stated, a man nam­
ed Mayer figured prominently, induc­
ing Mrs Ryder to leave her husband
early in December and accompany
him "east," as she has been assured.
Mrs. 'Miller has returned to her
home and, children and a reconcilia­
tion has been happily brought about,
according to reports. According to
the story, Mayer worked for Miller
during the fall and in some manner
gained a considerable influence over
the wife. She was indue 3d to depart
with him. taking her two little chil­
dren with her and some money, her
savings of many months.
At Minot Moyer is said to have left
h?r, he having first obtained posses­
sion of her savings and she went to
her parents at Drake, too humiliated
and heart-broken to return to the hus­
band she had deserted.
Mr. Millar, it is stated, went to
Drake and a reconciliation followed,
resulting in a happy family reunion
on Christmas day.

HAVE NARROW ESCAPE.
Napoleon, N. D., Jan. 9—J. Mohr,
residing near Kintyre, awoke with a
feeling of suffocation in the middle of
the night Christmas, and hurrying in­
to an adjoining bedroom, stumbled
over the body of his son, lying in a
stupor on the floor. Other members
of the family were more or less over­
come, but recovered promptly after
reacning the fresh air. It was dis
covered that coal gas, escaping from
1
the stove, had filled the ibedroorcn.
and the escape of the entire famil
from asphyxiation was due to the fa­
ther's being aroused in time.
Mrs. iMohr has been in feeble health
and her condition since the experi­
ence of Christmas night is serious.
The others are fully recovered.

Former Colonial Secretary of the
Oerman Empire Declares Sea
Is Free,
New York, Jan. 9.—That the sea is
common property of all the world and
in the maintenance of its freedom
means the cessation of war, was the
assertion by Ir. Bernhard Dernburg,
former colonial secretary of the Ger­
man empire, in an address today. The
present struggle, he declared, was a
fight "on one side for the absolute
dominion of seven seas, on the other
for a free sea. The
rEnglisJj
have
time and again asserted," he said,
"That Britannia mu3t rule the waves
as a matter of safety."
NEUTRALS SUPPORT
COUNTRTS DEMAND
United States Given Endorsement
in Communication With
England.
Washington, Jan. 9.—While no for­
mal announcement is made for com­
mon action by all the neutral coun­
tries regarding contraband in ship­
ping during maritime warfare, it was
announced that the. United States has
received the sympathetic support of
practically all the neutrals in negotia­
tions with England.
RELATIONS STRAIN­
ING WTH GREECE
(Continued from Pass One.)
Russians 'Repulsed.
iA! Berlin dispatch by wireless to Sa
ville says:
"The Austrian announcement says
the situation is generally unchanged
and there are no prolonged engage­
ments.
It was officially stated the Austri
ans have repulsed a strong .Russian
attack in the western Carpathians,
east of czeromka, capturing 400 pris­
oners and three machine guhs.
Germans Successful.
A iBerlin dispatch by wireless from
Saville says:
"The Germans are Anally successful
in fully reoccupying the Alsatian town
of Steinbach, says a dispatch from
Basel, Switzerland. The French forc­
es retreated to Thann, with heavy
losses.
Await Rumania.
Should Rumania, with the Russians
on the border of Transylvia, decidc
on entering the war with the Allies,
her army will provide the missing
link in a chain of troops winding from
the Baltic to the Adriatic, a distance
of over 1,000 miles. The battle line
would be composed of Russians, Ru­
manians, Servians and 'Montenegrins
on one side, and AustroHungarians
and Germans on the other. The Ailies
are confident Rumania will join them
soon.
Germans On Offensive.
There are important operations in
both the east and west. The Ger­
mans, simultaneously with renewal of
attacks on the left bank of the Vis­
tula, are developing a new offensive
in the direction of Mlawa on the east
Prussian frontier.
Operations Difficult.
A Montenegrin war office dispatch
saj's:
"Torrential rains and continued bad
weather renders all military opera­
tions difficult, but ,the {Montenegrins
firmly hold their positions, notwith­
standing the fierce Austrian artillery
fire."
IMPROVISED STILL DID WORK
Inventive Coolie Helped Medical Mis­
sionary Out of an Exceedingly
Tight Place.
In a life-and-death battle with a chol­
era epidemic in Hunan province,
China, in which an American medical
missionary was the leading figure, one
of the most serious difficulties was met
successfully by tine of the menial hos­
pital coolieB, according to a writer in
the World's Work. Since every pa­
tient had to have from one pint to sev­
eral quarts of distilled water with salt
In it, the water could not be distilled
fast enough. For two days the staff
struggled along with makeshifts while
thinking up a plan to build a still fotj
little money without the loss of timg.
This coolie solved the problem. In
one day and at a cost of less than two
dollars he built a stH) which later pro­
duced 20 gallons of distilled water
every 24 hours. I
The still was simplicity Itself. FVr
cooking food the Chinese use large flat
saucerlike pans, made of Iron. With
one of these febove and another below,
the coolie made a drum of galvanised
Iron with,a hole in the side for a
trough. Into the lower pan hot water
for boiling was poured. Underneath a
hot fire sent up a constant steam
vapor. The upper kettle was kept
cool by frequent changin'gB of cold wa«
ter. The vapor from the lower kettle,
condensed on the lower surface of the
cool upper kettle, dripped off into the
trough, and filled bottle after bottle
with distilled water.
